summary:
The Mississippi Bubble (1719–1720 AD) was a catastrophic financial collapse in France centered on John Law's Mississippi Company, which controlled trade with France's Louisiana territory and had been merged with the French national bank in an ambitious experiment to restructure state debt through paper money and corporate equity. Law, a Scottish financial theorist who had become France's finance minister (Contrôleur Général des Finances) after gaining the Regent Philippe d'Orléans's confidence, created a system in which Mississippi Company shares, backed by supposed Louisiana wealth, could be purchased with paper banknotes — inflating both the money supply and share prices to unsustainable levels. At its peak, Mississippi Company shares rose 3,600% before the collapse in 1720 left France's financial system in ruins.

The Mississippi Bubble's consequences for France proved more damaging than Britain's simultaneous [[event:2466|South Sea Bubble]]. The collapse destroyed confidence in paper money, banknotes, and joint-stock companies in France for nearly a century — hampering financial modernization and contributing to the fiscal inflexibility that would ultimately help precipitate the [[event:107|French Revolution]]. John Law fled France in disgrace and died in poverty in Venice in 1729. The Mississippi Bubble also left a lasting cultural mark: it is the origin of the English word 'millionaire' (coined during the boom when Mississippi shareholders became fabulously wealthy) and demonstrated the power of credit and paper money — both as tools of economic growth and as instruments of catastrophic instability.
